coredata.dc:title: One pot hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent NP-carbon dots derived from Dunaliella salina biomass and its application in on-off sensing of Hg (II), Cr (VI) and live cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Ankit Kumar
Singh, Vikas Kumar
Singh, Mamata
Singh, Prabhakar
Khadim, Sk. Riyazat
Singh, Urmilesh
Koch, Biplob
Hasan, S.H.
Asthana, R.K.
coredata.dc:description: The impact of hazardous chemicals and toxic metal ions in the environment played havoc to the ecosystem. In the present work green chemistry approach was applied for one-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen, phosphorus dual doped carbon dots, utilizing green precursor i.e, biomass of halophilic microalgae Dunaliella salina (algal derived nitrogen phosphorous carbon dots i.e A-NPCDs). Synthesized A-NPCDs were characterized through TEM, FT-IR, P-XRD, DLS and XPS. It showed appreciable optical properties with significant fluorescence quantum yield (8%)and exhibited blue color under UV – light (λex = 365 nm). A-NPCDs acted as fluorescent turn off sensor for toxic metal ion such as Hg (II) and Cr (VI) with good selectivity and sensitivity. Interestingly, ANPCDs detected Cr (VI) up to 0.018μM which was below the permissible level of Cr (VI) in drinking water. Such sensing resulted because of combination of inner filter effect and dynamic quenching mechanism. Moreover, it also showed good selectivity (0.018μM) for Hg (II) via dynamic quenching mechanism. MTT assay of A-NPCDs on HEK-293 cell line showed biocompatibility with negligible cytotoxicity. Therefore, these were successfully employed for live cell imaging and intracellular detection of Hg (II) and Cr (VI) in HEK-293 cell line. Thus, green synthesized A-NPCDs may be a good alternative for chemically derived CDs in intracellular detection of Hg (II) and Cr (VI) of a complex biological environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dunaliella salina
A-NPCDs
In-vitro cellular imaging
Hg (II)
Cr (VI) detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell imaging in HEK 293 cell line
Fluorescence turn off sensing of heavy toxic metal ions Hg (II) and Cr (VI)
Mechanism of fluorescence quenching of Hg (II) and Cr (VI)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Methodology adopted for the algal biomass derived hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen, phosphorus doped carbon dots (A-NPCDs).
Fluorescence quenching ofA-NPCDs in the presence of Hg (II) and Cr (VI) via electron transfer and inner filter effect respectively.
TEM micrograph of A-NPCDs (a), Average size distribution (b), SAED pattern (c), P-XRD pattern (d), Raman spectrum of synthesized A-NPCDs (e) and FT-IR spectra of as synthesized A-NPCDs (f).
Wide scan XPS survey spectrum (a), High resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (b), O 1 s (c), N 1 s (d) and P 2p of as synthesized A-NPCDs (e).

Absorption (black line) (a), excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of A-NPCDs with corresponding blue colour under UV-light, Excitation dependent emission spectra of synthesized A-NPCDs (b), CIE colour chromaticity diagram showing blue light emission with x, y coordinate of as synthesized A-NPCDs (c).

(A) Fluorescence titration experiment after addition of different concentration of Hg(II) (0–200 μM) in the 40 μg/mL of A-NPCDs (a), Corresponding linear calibration plot(b), Fluorescence titration experiment after incremental addition of Cr (VI) (0–150 μM) in the 40 μg/mL of A-NPCDs (c) and its linear calibration plot (d). 4 (B). Spectral overlapping between absorber Cr (VI) and fluorophore (A-NPCDs) showing IFE (a), Fluorescence decay profile in absence and presence of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) in A-NPCDs upon excitation at 340 nm (b).
Cell viability (MTT assay) in the presence of different level of A-NPCDs on HEK-293 (normal) cell line.

Cell imaging of HEK-293 cell line (A) normal phase image (B) fluorescence image in red channel (C) fluorescence image in green channel and (D) merge image of both red and green channel after treatment of 50 μg/mL of A-NPCDs for 24 h.
Cell imaging of HEK-293 cell line after treatment with 100 μM Hg (II) (A) normal phase image image (B) in red channel, (C) in green channel and (D) merged images in both channels.
Cell imaging of HEK-293 cell line after treatment with 100 μM Cr (VI) (A) normal phase image image (B) in red channel, (C) in green channel and (D) merged images in both channels.

head.ce:title: One pot hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent NP-carbon dots derived from Dunaliella salina biomass and its application in on-off sensing of Hg (II), Cr (VI) and live cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: R. N. Singh memorial Laboratory, Centre of Advanced study in Botany, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre of Advanced study in Botany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterial Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry I.I.T BHU, Varanasi 221005, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Zoology, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221 005, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Botany, Institute of Sciences, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi 221005, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The impact of hazardous chemicals and toxic metal ions in the environment played havoc to the ecosystem. In the present work green chemistry approach was applied for one-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen, phosphorus dual doped carbon dots, utilizing green precursor i.e, biomass of halophilic microalgae Dunaliella salina (algal derived nitrogen phosphorous carbon dots i.e A-NPCDs). Synthesized A-NPCDs were characterized through TEM, FT-IR, P-XRD, DLS and XPS. It showed appreciable optical properties with significant fluorescence quantum yield (8%)and exhibited blue color under UV – light (λex = 365 nm). A-NPCDs acted as fluorescent turn off sensor for toxic metal ion such as Hg (II) and Cr (VI) with good selectivity and sensitivity. Interestingly, ANPCDs detected Cr (VI) up to 0.018μM which was below the permissible level of Cr (VI) in drinking water. Such sensing resulted because of combination of inner filter effect and dynamic quenching mechanism. Moreover, it also showed good selectivity (0.018μM) for Hg (II) via dynamic quenching mechanism. MTT assay of A-NPCDs on HEK-293 cell line showed biocompatibility with negligible cytotoxicity. Therefore, these were successfully employed for live cell imaging and intracellular detection of Hg (II) and Cr (VI) in HEK-293 cell line. Thus, green synthesized A-NPCDs may be a good alternative for chemically derived CDs in intracellular detection of Hg (II) and Cr (VI) of a complex biological environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are the new class of zero dimension discrete quasi spherical nanomaterials with size less than 10 nm. CDs have tremendously attracted scientific community due to its outstanding physicochemical properties such as, tunable excitation emission properties, broad excitation spectra, low cost synthesis, less toxicity, good membrane permeability, biocompatibility, ecofriendly, excellent water solubility and easy surface functionalization ability [1], and broad application in various field including cell imaging [2,3], sensing of heavy toxic metal ions and biomolecules [4,5], drug delivery [6], solar cell and photo catalysis [7]. During last five years many of the research groups have synthesized carbon dots using varieties of chemical precursors with different methodology such as chemical oxidation [8], arc discharge [9], laser ablation [10], hydrothermal and microwave irradiation [11]. These methods were cost intensive and required toxic chemicals along with multi step synthesis making it more tedious. Therefore, the hydrothermal method using cheap and ecofriendly biomass derived precursor have gained more attention because of their green chemistry nature. The approach was cost effective because of low energy consumption, easy control of reaction, no use of toxic chemicals and self-passivation of CDs without any post treatment. At present several research groups have utilized a green precursor such as bamboos leaves [12], Sargassum fluitans [13], green tea [14], Prunus avium [15], Phyllanthus acidus [16], Magnolia liliflora [17] Durian shell waste [18], Osmium sanctum [19], Prunus persica [20] Hylocereus undatus [21], Chionanthus retusus [22] Garlic [23], and eutrophic algal bloom [24], for the synthesis of CDs, which showed various practical applications such as detection of toxic metal ions, bio imaging and drug delivery. Many of the research groups have been reported the detection of heavy toxic metal ion by carbon quantum dots such as Rao et al. [25] reported the detection of Hg (II) from chemically derived N-CDs, Libo et al. [26] synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dot-graphene oxide hybrid for the selective sensing of Cd (II) and Pb (II), Edison et al. [15] reported the detection of Fe (III) in aqueous medium form green synthesized N-CDs, Liu et al. [27] synthesized highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for detection of Cr (VI). Moreover, detection of heavy toxic metal ion through carbon dots is good for human welfare as well as environment because most of the conventional techniques required sophisticated instruments and trained operator which are expensive and time consuming process.

Among heavy metals Hg (II) is non-biodegradable highly toxic to human beings and converted to methyl mercury (highly toxic) by microorganisms in aquatic systems. Its bioacculation in food chain ultimately affects the human body even at very low concentration [28–30]. The mercury ion poisoning caused adverse effect on liver, kidney, cardiovascular and central nervous system of the human body [31]. On other hand, chromium is constantly released in water bodies from the leather industries and anthropogenic processes. Chromium showed variable oxidation states i.e. Cr (III) and Cr (VI) in aqueous system. Among these Cr (VI) is highly toxic to human health because of its great mobility and carcinogenic properties [32–35]. Therefore; it is urgent need to develop an efficient, cost effective and ecofriendly technique to detect these toxic metal ions at very low concentration in drinking water as well as in living cells.

In the present work it was demonstrated that a green precursor (algal biomass) and ecofriendly route (hydrothermal) synthesized nitrogen, phosphorous dual doped carbon dots (A-NPCDs) without using any chemical and passivating agent in one step method (Scheme 1 ). Dunaliella salina a unicellular wall-less alga isolated from Sambhar lake Rajasthan, India was cultivated in lab. This strain is halophilic in nature and has unique property to tolerate adverse environmental conditions such as high salinity, low temperature, high irradiance and nutrient limitations (micro and macronutrients). D. salina cells can accumulate large amount of ß-carotene [36] and glycerol [37] in specific conditions. In hypersaline conditions photosynthetic activity of D. sailna enhanced while in case of photosynthetic plants and microbes the photosynthetic efficiency decreased at similar salinity levels. Hasegawa et al. [38], reported that the growth of majority of plants inhibited at 0.2 M NaCl. Microalgal cultivation utilizes natural light, nonarable land, brackish or saline waters and minimal nutrients for growth and does not compete with agricultural farming [39]. Synthesized A-NPCDs were well dispersed in aqueous medium and showed excellent photo physical properties that led their practical applications in sensing of heavy toxic metal ions (Hg (II) and Cr (VI)) and cell imaging. Using various physical techniques it was documented that green synthesized carbon dots (A-NPCDs) showed multivariate application such as cell imaging and sensing of toxic metal ions Hg (II) and Cr (VI) in aqueous solution at physiological pH 6.8.

All the experimental materials procured from the commercial supplier and used as such without further purification. The whole experiment was carried out in double distilled water at ambient condition. The solution of different metal ions was prepared from their nitrate salt in double distilled water because nitrate salt is easily soluble in distilled water. The chromium solution was prepared from their potassium dichromate salt.

Dunaliella salina was isolated from sambhar lake Rajasthan and cultured in Modified Jonson Medium (MJM) [40]. The algal culture from the axenic stock were inoculated in 3 L flask containing MJM (1500 mL) with NaCl (0.5 M) and kept at 28 ± 2 °C under cool white fluorescent and tungsten light (14.4 Wm−2) with the light/dark cycle of 16/8 h. All the glassware’s and culture medium were sterilized at 121 °C (30 min) having pressure as 1.0546 kg cm−2 (15 lb) the transfer and mantainence of culture was performed aseptically under a laminar flow hood (INSTECH, India).

The algal biomass was harvested by centrifugation the culture at 4500 rpm for 4 min and washed twice with DDW to remove the medial constituents. 6.0 g wet algal biomass was dispersed it in 100 mL DDW and sonicated for 20 min. Afterwards, the suspension was transferred in a Teflon lined autoclave at 200 °Cfor 3 h. After completion of the reaction, the autoclave was cool naturally to room temperature. The obtained mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the larger particle and Supernatant was collected. The supernatant was further purified by dialysis membrane and stored at 4 °C for furthered use.

The synthesized A-NPCDs were characterized by using various analytical techniques that includes UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer, fluorescence spectrophotometer, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction(XRD) and DLS. The optical properties of the synthesized CDs were determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, Evolution 201) and emission spectra was carried out with (Agilent Technologies Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer) at different excitation wavelengths at fixed slid width of 5 nm. The size and morphology of A-NPCDs was observed through Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, TECNAI 20 G2) at accelerating voltage 200 kV. The Raman spectrum of A-NPCDs was recorded using a STR 300 Micro Raman Spectrometer having laser excitation at 532 nm. The presence of functional group was identified through FT-IR analysis on KBr pellet with PerkinElmer Spectrum 100 from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The elemental composition and surface functional state was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometer (XPS) (AMICUS, kratos, Analytical, A shimadzu) spectrometer with Mg Kα excitation (1253.6 eV) radiation. The Powder XRD data were collected on a RigakuSmart Lab between angle 2θ = 2-80°. Zeta potential and was performed using Malvern zeta sizer Nano apparatus equipped with a 4.0 mW laser. The fluorescence decay profile was obtained on picoseconds time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) instrument from Edinburg FLS 900 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The quantum yield of the as synthesized A-NPCQDs was measured with reference to the quinine Sulphate (QY = 54%). The absorbance (below 0.1) and integrated intensity of quinine sulphate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4was measured at excitation of 340 nm, at the same parameter absorbance and integrated intensity of A-NPCDs was monitored and quantum yield determine by use data listed in (ESI table S2) with equation (ESI equation1).

The detection of metal ions was carried out as follow: The stock solution of 0.01 M of Hg (II) and Cr (VI) solution was prepared by mixing 34.2 mg of nitrate and 29.4 mg dichromate salt of mercury and chromium in 10 mL distilled water respectively. The desired concentration of metal ions was obtained through serial dilution of stock solution. Afterward the fluorescence titration spectra were carried out for both the metal ion in the absence and presence of different concentration of metal ions in A-NPCDs. The limit of detection was calculated using the formula 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation and m is the slope of the (F0/F Vs C) graph. The interference of other metal ion was checked by similar manner.

HEK 293 human embryonic kidney normal cell lines were grown in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% FBS, 100 U mL−1 penicillin and 100 mg mL−1 streptomycin solutions in the humidified CO2 incubator at 37 °C.

Cell viability was carried out using a colorimetric assay which is based on the conversion of the yellow tetrazolium salt MTT (3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) to purple colour formazan crystals by reaction with mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase of metabolically active cells. Cytotoxic activities of A-NPCDs dots formulation were determined on HEK-293 (normal human embryonic kidney cell line).The cell line was maintained in high glucose complete DMEM (complete medium consisting 10% fetal bovine serum, supplemented with 20 mM l-glutamine, 100 U mL−1 penicillin and 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin) at 37 °C and 5% CO2 in a humidified atmosphere. Briefly, 1 × 104 HEK-293 cells per well were seeded and incubated for 24 h for adherence. After 24 h, the span medium was replaced by fresh DMEM (Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium) containing various concentrations of A-NP-CDs formulation followed by incubation for 24 h in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere. The medium was then removed and 100 μL of fresh medium containing 5 mg mL−1MTT solution was added to each well and incubated further for 2 h. Finally, the medium containing MTT was removed and 100 μL DMSO were added into each well to dissolve the formazan crystal and incubated in dark at 37ºC for 30 min. The intensity of the colour developed was assayed using a Micro ELISA plate reader at 570 nm and the cell viability was estimated according to the following formula.
%
C e l l
V i a b i l i t y =
O D o f t r e a t e d c e l l s
O D o f c o n t r o l c e l l s
X 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging in HEK 293 cell line
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake of the A-NPCDs was observed using fluorescent microscopy. Normal human embryonic kidney cell line (HEK- 293) were seeded in 96 well plate with a density of 1 × 104 cells per well. After 24 h, the seeded cells were treated with 100 μL (50 μg mL−1) of the fresh medium containing A-NPCDs formulation and incubated further for 24 h at 37ºCin humidified 5% CO2 incubator. The cells were then washed thrice by phosphate buffered saline (PBS) images of HEK-293 cells were taken in an inverted fluorescent microscope (Evos FL, Life technologies) in both red and green channel.

Synthesized carbon dots were analyzed through transmission electron microscopy revealed spherical morphology and size varied from 2 to 8 nm with an average of 4.7 nm (Fig. 1 a, b and ESI Fig. S6). The selected area electron diffraction pattern indicated crystalline nature of A-NPCDs, and may be ascribed to halophilic nature of the microalgae (Fig. 1c). The P-XRD pattern showed a broad peak at 2θ = 19.8° is consistent with the presence of amorphous nature of carbon dots, however sharp pattern with strong reflection at 2θ = 31, 46 and 56 confirmed the presence of sodium salt (Fig. 1d) [41]. To investigate the defect in carbon nanomaterial Raman spectrum of A-NPCDs was carried out which revealed two distinct peaks at 1354 and 1581 cm−1 (Fig. 1e) attributed to D and G band respectively [42]. The D band is related to defect in the graphitic sheet of carbon dots whereas; G band corresponded to the vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atom of graphitic sheet [43]. The intensity of D and G band are represented as ID and IG respectively and the ratio of the peak intensity (ID /IG ) determined the degree of graphitization of carbon nanomaterials. The moderate intensity ratio (0.85) of D and G band (ID <IG ) of A-NPCDs indicated the graphitic structure without any carboneous impurities [15]. FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots ( Fig. 1f) revealed the presence of various functional group such as peak at 3393 cm-1 (OH stretching), 3272 and 3063 cm-1 (NH stretching), 2927 cm−1 (sp2-CH stretching), 2851 cm−1 (sp3 -C-H stretching), 1668 cm−1 (CO stretching of amide), 1516 cm−1 (NH bending), 1440 cm-1 (-C = C stretching), 1379 cm−1(CO Stretching), 1152 cm-1 (CN/PN stretching) and peak at 697 cm−1 indicated the presence of phosphate group [44,45]. Besides, the zeta potential was found to be (-5.31 mV) ascertained the presence of negatively charged functional group on the surface of carbon dots (ESI Fig. S1).To elucidate the elemental composition and surface functional state x-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was obtained (Fig. 2 a) indicated the presence of O1 s (530.61 eV), N1 s (400.72 eV), C1 s (285.19 eV), P2s (184.24 eV) and P2p (131.31 eV) respectively. In addition to elemental analysis such spectra showed the composition as carbon (52.2%), oxygen (42.9%) nitrogen (2.6%) and phosphorus (2.2%). However, high resolution C 1 s spectrum deconvulated into different carbon states including CC/CC (283.3 eV), CN/CP/ (284.7 eV) and CO/CO (286.2 eV) (Fig. 2b).The high resolution O1 s spectrum of carbon dots ( Fig. 2c) decomposed into four surface component COC (530.4 eV), PO (531.0 eV), COH (531.52 eV), C = O (532.15 eV) [46]. However quantitative fitting of high resolution N 1 s exhibited two bonding states C-N/ Pyrrolic N (398.0 eV) and N-H (398.74 eV) respectively (Fig. 2d). The high resolution P2p deconvulated into two peak P-C/P-N (131.6 eV) and P-O (132.5 eV) (Fig. 2e) [46–48]. The presence of pyrrolic nitrogen and phosphate group on the surface of CDs clearly indicated that abundance of amino acids and protein of algal biomass successfully doped in to the lattice of carbon dots without using any external passivation agent which is economical and ecofriendly. The presence of heteroatom nitrogen and phosphorous enhanced the electronic properties, quantum yield, hydrophilicity and generate more active site on the surface of A-NPCDs which enhances the materialistic application of synthesized A-NPCDs in various fields including sensing of toxic heavy metal ion, bioimaging and drug delivery.

Optical properties of synthesized A-NPCDs were analyzed using UV–vis absorption spectra which showed weakly resolved bands at 275 nm and 328 nm (Fig. 3 a, black line). The absorption band at 275 nm ascribed due to π-π* transition of sp2hybridized aromatic CC bond i.e. core of the carbon dots. Band position at 328 nm arose because of n-π* transition of heterogeneous surface functional groups of the A-NPCDs especially due to CO and COH moieties [49]. The corresponding excitation spectra (Fig. 3a red line) exhibited two excitation maxima at 240 nm and 358 nm which was well consisted with absorption band and ensured that 240 nm peak attributed to the π-π* transition of the core of A-NPCDs while 358 nm correspond to n-π* transition of surface moieties [50]. The aqueous solution of A-NPCDs showed blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation at 365 nm (inset Fig. 3a). The blue colour fluorescence was confirmed by Commission Internationaledel'Éclairage (CIE) 1931 chromaticity diagram obtained from the fluorescence data with CIE coordinate (0.20, 0.19) (Fig. 3c) [51]. Further synthesized A-NPCDs showed excitation dependent emission behaviors like most of the luminescent carbon nanomaterials with maximum emission at 415 nm of excitation wavelength 340 nm (Fig. 3b). Thus, we have selected 340 nm of excitation wavelength throughout experiment. As the excitation wavelength increased from 340 nm to 400 nm, emission intensity decreased with bathochromic shift of 60 nm (415 nm to 475 nm). The decrease in intensity with bathochromic shift indicated that heterogeneous functional group (-OH, NH2, CONH2, and PO4 3−) on the surface of CDs. This is explained as defective surface leads bathochromic shift rather than other possible effect [52,53]. Heterogeneous surface agents of A-NPCDs caused appreciable fluorescence quantum yield (8%) with respect to the quinine sulphate (QY 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4) (ESI table S1).

To monitor the application of synthesized carbon dots, the effect of pH, ionic strength and exposure time on their fluorescence properties was investigated. Impact of pH on their emission spectra was observed by varying pH from 2 to 12 with addition of 0.1 M HCl and NaOH solution to the aqueous solution of A-NPCDs. The emission properties were changed with varying the pH and showed maximum intensity in pH range 3–11 (ESI Fig. S2).This observation inferred that A-NPCDs may be used in a broad pH range (3–11). It was supported by photograph under UV-light (λex = 365 nm) at different pH. Effect of ionic strength was also investigated by treating the A-NPCDs with different concentrations of sodium chloride solution (0.2–5 M) and interestingly there was almost no change in the emission intensity, even at high concentration of NaCl solution supporting the view that the surface functional groups of the CDs were non ionizable and remained stable under high salt stress (ESI Fig. S3). Evaluation of its photostbility A-NPCDs solution was done by irradiating under UV-light at 365 nm for 50 h and there was no effect on their fluorescence intensity which ensured that A-NPCDs possessed strong photoluminescence properties (ESI Fig. S4). In addition to the photo stability, aqueous solution of the A-NPCDs was in a homogeneous phase up to 120 d showing long term stability of the synthesized A-NPCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence turn off sensing of heavy toxic metal ions Hg (II) and Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Screening of sensing behavior of synthesized A-NPCDs was investigated. Experiment was carried out by using 50 μL of 2.0 mM solution of Na+, K+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, As3+, Pb2., and Ag+ were added to the 1.0 ml aqueous solution of A-NPCDs at pH 6.8 followed by fluorescence spectra (ESI Fig. S5). Effective quenching was observed in the presence of Hg (II) and Cr (VI), while other metal ions have negligible effect suggesting that A-NPCDs was insensitive for other metal ions in aqueous solution and selectively detected the highly toxic Hg (II) and Cr (VI) without interference of other metal ions. The origin of selectivity arises because Cr (VI) had broad absorption band which effectively overlapped the emission and excitation spectrum of A-NPCDs i.e. the blue color of A-NPCDs was consumed by addition of Cr (VI) solution resulting quenching of the fluorescence of A-NPCDs. Although, Hg (II) had higher affinity to bind with sulphur atom due to soft-soft interaction, however, Hg (II) readily binds with nitrogen, phosphorus and oxygen donor atoms. Since the surface of A-NPCDs possessed nitrogen, phosphorus and oxygen containing functional groups which readily interacted with Hg (II) and facilitated the electron transfer from A-NPCDs to Hg (II), resulting quenching of their fluorescence. Therefore, the synthesized A-NPCDs may be exclusively utilized for the selective sensing of Hg (II) and Cr (VI) in the mixture of other metal ions in aqueous system.

In order to decipher sensing behavior of A-NPCDs, fluorescence titration experiment was carried out in the presence of Hg (II) and Cr (VI). The bare solution of A-NPCDs was excited at 340 nm exhibited emission at 415 nm but after gradual addition of Hg (II) from 0 to 200 μM emission intensity decreased without negligible shift in the band position. This may be explained on the basis of electrostatic interaction between negatively charged surface functional groups and positively charged metal ions leading sensitivity towards the Hg (II) detection Fig. 4 A(a). Similarly, gradual addition of Cr (VI) solution (0–150 μM) led decrease in emission intensity corresponding to 415 nm without significant change in the band position ascertaining that A-NPCDs was active toward Cr (VI) solution Fig. 4A(c). Thus, algal biomass derived CDs was a good candidate for turn off detection of Hg (II) and Cr (VI) contaminated water bodies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence quenching of Hg (II) and Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para:
The quenching of fluorescence of A-NPCD in the presence of metal ions may arise due to various reasons such as electron transfer from CDs to the empty orbital’s of metal ions, inner filter effect (IFE), coordination induced aggregation (CIA), static and dynamic quenching may be due to occurrence of forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and metal ions or simultaneous happening of the two [54]. The phenomenon of inner filter effect (IFE) occurs when absorption spectra of the absorber (metal ions) precisely overlap to the excitation or emission spectra of fluorophore materials (CDs).There was no significant peak in the absorption spectra of Hg (II) (black line) Fig. 4B(a) hence the occurrence of IFE in the fluorescence quenching of A-NPCDs was ruled out. However an aqueous solution of positively charged Hg (II) was gradually added to A-NPCDs (negatively charged), an effective electron transfer process occurred from A-NPCDs to Hg (II) facilitating the non-radiative electron/hole recombination and annihilation i.e. the excited state electron transfer from the A-NPCDs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital’s (LUMO) of the Hg (II) leading to quenching FL intensity of A-NPCDs [55,56]. Further confirmation of the quenching mechanism was done using fluorescence life time decay in absence and presence of Hg (II) on time correlation single photon counting (TCSPC) method. The fluorescence decay profile (Fig. 4B(b) and ESI table S2) was well fitted by triexponential function and the average life time was calculated as 2.81 ns for bare A-NPCDs and 1.33 ns in the presence of Hg (II). Thus, the change in life time of A-NPCDs after addition of Hg (II) strongly supported the excitate state interaction i.e. dynamic quenching mechanism [57,58]. The change in fluorescence intensity with the concentration of Hg (II) i.e. quenching efficiency was well fitted by Stern-Volmer Eq. (1).
(1)
F o
F
= 1 + K s v
Q
Where Fo and F are fluorescence intensity in absence and presence of Hg (II). Q is the concentration of Hg (II) and Ksv is quenching constant. From the Stern-Volmer equation quenching constant was calculated as1.74 × 106 L mol−1 with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9998. Moreover, a linear relation was observed at 0.03-0.20 μM L−1 and limit of detection was calculated as 0.018 μM using formula 3σ/m with signal to noise ratio 3 (Fig.4A (b)).

Similarly quenching mechanism of A-NPCDs by Cr (VI) was established as absorption spectra of Cr (VI) (yellow line) at 258 nm 354 nm and 439 nm, while the excitation (red line) at 241 nm and 352 nm and emission spectra (blue line) at 425 nm of A-NPCDs. The good and précised spectral overlapping of absorber Cr (VI) and fluorophore A-NPCDs strongly favored the occurrence of inner filter effect resulting the light emitted by fluorophore (A-NPCDs) consumed by absorber Cr (VI) leading to quenching of the blue colour [59]. Additional evidence of quenching mechanism emerged through fluorescence life time decay as calculated for Hg (II). The average life time of A-NPCDs (2.81 ns) decrease on addition of Cr (VI) (1.11 ns) (From the Fig. 4B (b))and ESI table S2) indicated the excitation state interaction between A-NPCDs and Cr (VI) i.e., dynamic quenching mechanism hence the synergistic effect of IFE and dynamic quenching led to fluorescence quenching of blue colour of A-NPCDs [60]. Quantitative estimation of the change in fluorescence intensity with concentration of Cr (VI) was investigated and well fitted by Stern-Volmer equation. The quenching constant Ksv was calculated as 8.80 × 105 L mol−1 having correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.988). The linear relation was obtained from (0.03 - 0.18 μM L-1) and limit of detection was calculated as 0.018 μM with signal to noise ratio 3 (Fig. 4A(d)).The limit of detection in both the cases Hg (II) and Cr (VI) were comparable to already reported biologically derived CDs. The mechanistic view is represented in Scheme 2 .

Proposed microalgal (D. salina) biomass derived A-NPCDs was further employed for in vitro cell imaging in absence and presence of these toxic metal ions on HEK-293 cell line. Prior to cell imaging processes cytotoxicity of A-NPCDs was checked by treating the HEK-293 cell line with 5–75 μg mL−1 of A-NPCDs. Approximately 80% cells were viable even up to high dosage (75 μg mL−1) of A-NPCDs after 24 h incubation time (Fig. 5 ) suggesting that the synthesized A-NPCDs showed negligible cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

In summary, it was demonstrated that green precursor approach (algal biomass of D.salina) and ecofriendly route for one pot hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen, phosphorus dual doped carbon dots (A-NPCDs) without using any chemical and passivating agent. Synthesized A-NPCDs was characterized by various instrumental techniques such as TEM, FT-IR, P-XRD, XPS, UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The excellent optical properties and significant fluorescent quantum yield possessing A-NPCDs which acted as a (on-off) sensor for the heavy toxic metal ion such as Hg (II) and Cr (VI) with good selectivity and sensitivity at pH 6.8. The limit of detection for Cr (VI) was found to be 0.018 μM (0.9 μg L−1) which was below the permissible level in drinking water (50 μg L−1) while in case of Hg limit of detection was calculated to be 0.018 μM better than already reported biologically derived sensor for Hg. The mechanistic studies suggested that A-NPCDs displayed the dual mode mechanisms towards sensing of these metal ions. The inner filter effect and dynamic quenching played a vital role the case of Cr (VI) detection because absorption band of Cr (VI) overlapped precisely to excitation/ emission band of fluorophore (A-NPCDs) along with decrease in the fluorescence life time in presence of Cr (VI). Sensing of Hg (II) involved only dynamic quenching process. In addition to this, MTT assay of the A-NPCDs showed outstanding biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity, therefore it may be potentially employed for live cell imaging in presence and absence of Hg (II) and Cr (VI), showing appreciable results in dual channel (green and red).Thus, the proposed algal biomass derived A-NPCDs will be a better option for detection of Hg and Cr (VI) over chemically derived CDs to detect Hg (II) and Cr (VI) contaminated cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge Head and Program Coordinator, CAS in Botany, BHU, DST-FIST/PURSE, ISLS BHU for research facilities and UGC-UPE II for partial funding. Prof. ON Srivastava Department of Physics (for XRD) and Dr. Ashish Kumar Mishra Department of Material Science Indian Institute of Technology (B.H.U) for Raman Spectroscopy. The financial supports are also acknowledged by AKS (22/12/2013(ii) EU-V) to UGC, SRK (09/013/(0574)/2015-EMR-I), US(09/013(0597)/2015-EMR-I) to CSIR, New Delhi; PS(R/DEV./D./DST-PURSE/5050/20244) to DST-PURSE.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2019.02.023.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
